title: TBM 416: Investment Stewardship (As Habit)
questions: What are we actually getting from our engineering and product investments before it’s too late?
How do we measure engineering ROI when outcomes are lagged and causality is several hops away from dollars?
Why do common metrics like revenue per engineer or story points often mislead leadership decisions?
How should we decide whether to hire more engineers versus improving leverage with the team we already have?
search_terms: stingy about hiring watchful supportive teams
summary: The post argues that engineering ROI is not a one-time calculation but a long-term set of stewardship behaviors that must be built before leadership asks the hard question. It targets engineering/product leaders who struggle with misleading metrics, lagging outcomes, and the temptation to cold-start ROI analysis using benchmarks or vanity measures. The proposed remedy is continuous confidence updating with leading indicators, honest finance modeling, and disciplined hiring focused on leverage rather than headcount.

title: TBM 403: The Seduction (And Folly) Of Rollups, Points, and (Most) Time Tracking
questions: Why do rollups and portfolio trees fail to reflect reality in modern product organizations?
When do story points and time tracking become misleading or counterproductive?
How can leaders make systems observable without relying on precise but false metrics?
What should teams measure instead of chasing “add up” numbers and perfect reporting structures?
search_terms: How to Measure Anything decision-based measurement
summary: This post argues that executives’ desire for neat rollups using story points and time tracking often produces misleading “faux accounting” because knowledge work doesn’t fit stable, predefined categories. It’s aimed at product, engineering, and product-ops leaders who need to make organizations observable and steer investment, but want to avoid metric gaming and proxy-driven management. The alternative proposed is to measure signals that match the system and the decisions being made, emphasizing outcomes and capacity-relevant indicators over perfect reporting structures.

title: TBM 249: Return on Investment (and Allocation)
questions: How do we answer where we are investing our money and what we are getting for it in r&d?
Why do time tracking and utilization-based roi calculations lead to bad incentives and worse outcomes?
Should we measure roi by output (projects shipped) or by outcomes and learning?
How can we calculate roi when work is collaborative and full of dependencies?
summary: This post argues that measuring ROI in product development is often derailed by spreadsheet-driven false precision, time tracking, and proxy metrics that create bad incentives. It is aimed at product and engineering leaders who need a more systems-oriented way to think about investment, focusing on teams, actionable inputs, throughput, and learning loops rather than controllable-but-misleading outputs or revenue attribution. It also warns that in low-trust, systemic dysfunction situations, better math won’t fix the underlying organizational problems.

title: TBM 241: Convictions (Loosely Held)
questions: How much conviction do product teams need when causality is hard to prove?
What do you do when the metric you want to optimize for long-term outcomes is hard to define?
How can you test strategic assumptions like flywheels and network effects without perfect measurement?
How should teams balance short-term revenue signals with long-term quality and second-order effects?
search_terms: second and third order effects product quality
willingness to be proven wrong product teams
summary: This post contrasts easy-to-measure e-commerce performance tweaks with harder-to-quantify B2B SaaS strategic bets like quality, flywheels, and second/third-order effects. It argues that product teams need “convictions loosely held”—enough belief to make leaps of faith, paired with a strong willingness to be proven wrong—because long-term value rarely has a perfect metric. It’s aimed at product managers and teams trying to decide what to measure and how to experiment when causality and outcomes are uncertain.

title: TBM 1/52: Product Manage Your Meetings
questions: What would we observe if a specific meeting were working as designed?
How do we distinguish meeting motion (how it runs) from meeting purpose (why it exists)?
Which meetings lack clear desired outcomes, and how can we redesign or eliminate them?
When is a meeting the wrong tool, and what alternative approach could achieve the outcome better?
summary: The post argues that teams should treat meetings like products: define desired outcomes, identify observable success signals, and design the meeting “motion” to reliably produce those outcomes. It’s aimed at product managers and leaders who want to improve organizational decision-making and reduce wasted time by assigning ownership, using measurement thinking, and redesigning or eliminating ineffective meetings.

title: TBM 56/52: The NPS Effect
questions: Why does NPS persist in companies even when it has known flaws?
How do social comparison and bandwagon effects shape adoption of customer metrics?
What organizational biases make it hard to replace NPS with better measurement approaches?
How does professional identity and sunk cost reinforce metric entrenchment?
summary: This post reframes Net Promoter Score (NPS) as a case study for how seemingly flawed business metrics become entrenched through social dynamics and cognitive biases. It’s for leaders, analysts, and data practitioners who want to understand why NPS sticks in organizations and what mechanisms drive metric persistence.

title: TBM 42/52: Outcome Super Prompt
questions: How do we help teams focus on what they’re measuring rather than how they’ll measure it?
What does a good driver statement look like in a fill-in-the-blank template?
How can we specify outcomes precisely using constrained choices for variable definitions?
How do we connect initiatives to drivers and outcomes through a traceable structure?
search_terms: A B C D metrics prompt
Miro workshop activity drivers constraints floats
summary: The post presents a facilitation activity (“outcome super prompt”) for helping teams write specific driver/outcome statements using a structured A-B-C-D template. It’s aimed at product and growth teams who want to clarify what they’re measuring (and for whom) before deciding how to measure it, with an optional advanced step to trace from initiatives up to root drivers.

title: TBM 29A/52: The Vanity Metric Cure: Context, Intent, and Action
questions: How do I tell whether a metric is a vanity metric or a useful, actionable metric?
What does it mean to align metrics with intent and context?
How can my team pressure test the metrics we rely on?
How should we communicate about metrics without getting trapped by misleading framing?
search_terms: SEO title vs real content metrics
summary: This post is for practitioners who use metrics at work and want to avoid vanity metrics by focusing on intent, context, and effectiveness. It reframes a vanity-metrics discussion into a practical approach, including a team activity to pressure test the metrics you rely on.

title: TBM 9/52: When Nothing/Everything (Actually) Works...
questions: How do vanity and proxy metrics prevent teams from learning what actually works?
What causes teams to get stuck in a loop of doing more without understanding impact?
How can teams create feedback signals that motivate rather than deflate?
What’s the difference between inertia and real learning from experiments?
summary: The post argues that when teams can’t tell whether initiatives are working, they fall into a cycle of escalating effort, chasing new strategies, and relying on vanity/proxy metrics that obscure learning. It’s aimed at product and delivery leaders and team members who want clearer feedback signals to reduce cognitive load, avoid success theater, and build a culture where both positive and negative outcomes motivate iteration.

title: TMB 5/52: Powerful Ideas > Perfect Measures
questions: How do you measure ideas that are hard to quantify, like trust or flexibility?
What is minimally viable measurement for powerful but intangible business goals?
How can you test a hypothesis when the key impact happens outside your product?
How should you measure content quality and connect it to acquisition and retention?
summary: The post argues that teams should not wait for perfect metrics to act on high-leverage “powerful ideas.” It recommends minimally viable measurement methods—like structured interviews and coarse-grained classification—so hypotheses about intangibles, second-order effects, and content quality can be tested using downstream business outcomes. It’s aimed at product, data, and analytics practitioners who need practical ways to measure what’s difficult to measure.

title: TBM 2/52: Leaps of Product Faith
questions: How can a CPO be confident in a product strategy without relying on blind faith?
What should product leaders do when long-term impact can’t be fully quantified?
How do you avoid metric paralysis while still using metrics and experiments responsibly?
How can product leaders build and sustain credibility for long-term bets?
summary: The post argues that long-term product strategy requires “informed faith” when perfect metrics and models for future impact aren’t available. It’s aimed at product leaders and CPOs who need to balance experimentation and measurement with narrative-building, credibility, and alignment across the executive team. It also offers a practical starting point: create a mini-model using leading indicators and testable hypotheses to begin earning trust for bigger bets.

title: TBM 51/52: Make "Using Data" Easy
questions: How do we make using data low-friction for product and analytics teams?
What causes dashboards to go un-viewed and how can we increase engagement?
Do we need centralized experts or upfront alignment to instrument and run experiments effectively?
How can we maintain data quality over time and avoid dashboard entropy?
search_terms: instrumentation as part of every story
summary: This post argues that teams adopt analytics and experimentation only when using data is low-friction—fast, collaborative, and integrated into everyday work. It’s aimed at product, engineering, and analytics leaders who want to reduce ritualized instrumentation overhead, prevent dashboard decay, and improve trust and engagement through practices like weekly collaboration, notifications, and data maintenance.

title: TBM 38/52: 15 Behaviors of Healthy, Data-Informed Teams
questions: What behaviors distinguish healthy, data-informed product teams?
How do teams build data trust and data literacy across onboarding and collaboration?
What does it mean to measure to learn instead of to keep score?
How should teams instrument products and run experiments under uncertainty?
search_terms: measure to learn vs keep score
data usability naming conventions data dictionaries
summary: This post outlines observable behaviors of healthy, data-informed product teams, aimed at product leaders, analytics practitioners, and teams trying to improve how they use data. It emphasizes skepticism, collaborative analysis, embedded instrumentation, data usability, insight reuse, and learning-oriented measurement. The guidance is framed as practical team practices and operating norms for making better decisions and experimenting safely under uncertainty.

title: TBM 33/52: Challenge Proxies
questions: How far is our team from the things that matter, and where are we using stand-ins?
What assumptions and imagination leaps are we making when we treat a proxy as evidence of customer impact?
Why are we using this proxy, and what would we do if the proxy changes?
Where is waste piling up because the organization measures the wrong thing (or measures indirectly)?
search_terms: trust proxies firm commitments go-between manager
summary: This post argues that software product teams frequently rely on stand-ins—proxies—for customer reality, strategy, and trust, and that these proxies can make waste invisible. It’s for product and engineering leaders who want a practical habit of questioning why a proxy is used, how strong its causal link is, and how to reduce indirect measurement and coordination when possible.

title: TBM 32/52: The Form That Stole 20,531,250 Seconds
questions: How can a form end up wasting millions of seconds without anyone noticing?
What should teams do differently when they discover high validation error rates?
Why do even talented designers and teams miss usability problems for years?
What are the fastest ways to find and fix broken UX in existing products?
search_terms: lost time due to broken UX
summary: The post argues that teams can unknowingly ship long-running UX problems—like forms that fail validation at high rates—until they instrument workflows and analyze submission errors. It’s aimed at product and design practitioners who want practical ways to uncover issues (measurement, moderated usability testing) and to understand why organizational factors can block learning and ownership. The takeaway is that many usability improvements are achievable quickly once teams gain situational awareness and make insights actionable.

title: TBM 19/52: The Analytics ROI Paradox
questions: Why is analytics ROI so hard to communicate to leadership?
How does lack of situational awareness make decision-quality improvements feel abstract?
What organizational behaviors (batching, reflection, pivoting) drive better outcomes?
How can a change agent justify investment in analytics tooling when teams feel they are already busy shipping?
summary: The post explores why organizations struggle to justify analytics and insight tooling: without situational awareness of impact, improving decision quality feels distant and leadership defaults to output and proxy measures. It’s aimed at change agents and product/engineering leaders trying to persuade teams to invest in analytics by reframing ROI as marginal gains that compound across thousands of decisions, especially when paired with smaller batches, early pivots, and reflection.

title: TBM 2/52: Start by Counting Things
questions: How do I choose the right metrics when I'm afraid of picking the wrong ones?
What should we measure first in a product to reduce uncertainty?
How do we instrument events and properties without overengineering?
How can we move from occasional analytics to measurement integrated into product development?
summary: The post argues that product teams should begin analytics by instrumenting and counting meaningful events, properties, and workflows tied to product promises and key value exchanges, rather than trying to identify perfect KPIs or OKRs immediately. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams stuck in metric-selection anxiety, offering a practical approach to reduce uncertainty and build measurement into everyday work through iterative experimentation.

title: TBM 53/53: Virtuous Improvement Loops
questions: Why do maturity models and grades often fail to produce real improvement?
What does a virtuous improvement loop look like for data-informed decision making?
How do you scale data literacy and data access to decision makers and teams?
What variables should we connect to design an amplifying improvement cycle in a specific area?
summary: The post argues that organizations should stop relying on grades or static maturity assessments and instead build virtuous, reinforcing improvement loops in sociotechnical systems. It’s aimed at product and analytics leaders who want practical guidance for creating a feedback cycle connecting usable data, data literacy, decision quality, and measurable outcomes. The author provides a checklist-like framing and an exercise for mapping variables to generate “flywheel” momentum.

title: TBM 50/53: The Curse of "Success" Metrics
questions: Why do talented teams struggle to pick success metrics for strategic, long-lived initiatives?
How do metrics translate into team and individual goals in ways that create fear of choosing the wrong measure?
What’s the difference between measuring to learn and measuring to control, and how does it change outcomes?
How can we start with uncertain metrics and safely iterate toward better cause/effect understanding?
search_terms: measuring to learn vs measuring to control
uncertainty and cause/effect in product metrics
iterative metric design what vs how
summary: The post explains why product teams often fail to select effective “success metrics” for strategic initiatives, even when they can map product beliefs. It argues that metrics become entangled with prioritization, goal-setting, and performance scrutiny, leading teams to avoid uncertain cause/effect and default to feature-factory behavior. It offers guidance for making measurement safe for learning by starting with what you know, iterating the measurement approach, and explicitly separating learning metrics from control metrics.

title: TBM 49/53: Signals and Confidence
questions: Which observable signals along the customer journey predict loyalty and high LTV?
How can we make and communicate our assumptions about customer behavior without pretending we know everything?
What should we instrument in the product to test these signals?
How do we move from intuition to evidence using correlation and causation analysis?
summary: This post presents a lightweight workshop activity for teams to map observable customer signals onto key milestones in a customer journey. It’s designed to help product and analytics teams surface uncertain assumptions about what predicts loyalty and high lifetime value, then translate those assumptions into measurable events to instrument and test. The approach is especially useful when teams struggle to discuss uncertainty and want a fast way to identify what to measure.

title: TBM 45/53: Rank Your 2020 Initiatives (by Impact)
questions: How can we rank product initiatives by impact when we don’t have perfect data?
What is the minimum measurement needed to make better product decisions in B2B?
How do we handle cases where we can’t tell whether initiatives were net positive?
When should we run learning reviews to capture real customer outcomes?
search_terms: qualitative customer interviews for product learning
summary: The post offers a practical exercise for product teams to rank prior year initiatives by impact, using the resulting uncertainty as a prompt for better decision-making. It is aimed at B2B product leaders who want to improve prioritization without relying on heavy experimentation, by using lightweight measurement (retention, task efficacy, interviews) and by running learning reviews after customer effects settle.

title: TBM 32/53: Beyond Generic KPIs
questions: What is the right north star metric for a product beyond generic KPIs like DAU and MAU?
How do you define a metric in a way that captures the human value story of the product?
When should you use standard engagement ratios like DAU/MAU versus custom outcome metrics?
How can metric definitions evolve without losing strategic focus?
search_terms: weekly active users vs outcome metrics
summary: The post argues that while standard KPIs like DAU and MAU can indicate engagement, they often fail to communicate the actual product bet and human value. It presents a human-centered north star metric—weekly learning users—defined around learning and sharing outcomes, and explains why custom metrics can better align teams with strategy. It’s aimed at product and analytics leaders who want to design metrics that inspire action and can evolve as assumptions change.

title: TBM 17/53: Measuring to Learn vs. Measuring to Conform
questions: How can we tell whether our measurement system is helping us learn or pushing us to conform and justify?
When should we demand proof (e.g., A/B tests) versus when should we accept uncertainty and iterate?
What happens when teams spend months perfecting success metrics—are we learning or just optimizing a hypothesis?
How do we balance empowered risk-taking with organizational requirements for proof?
search_terms: measuring to learn vs measuring to justify
risk governance proof vs bold bets
summary: This post argues that measurement practices often blur the line between learning and compliance—leading teams to overfit to metrics, demand proof prematurely, and slow down strategy discovery. It’s aimed at product teams and analytics leaders who want clearer measurement governance, stronger data literacy, and a pragmatic approach to when to use experiments versus iterative learning under uncertainty.
